title: If history is any indication, expect some goals in the World Cup final between Argentina and Spain
content:
In the 22 previous World Cup men's finals (which would include the 1950 match between Uruguay and Brazil that technically wasn’t a final, but decided who won the World Cup), the winning team has averaged 2.6 goals in the title matchup. The losing team has averaged 1.1 goals.
The two most recent World Cup finals have been offensive slugfests, with a combined 12 goals. France beat Croatia 4-2 in 2018, while Argentina and France tied 3-3 in 2022 (before Argentina prevailed in a penalty shootout).
Teams scoring at least two goals have won the World Cup title 17 times; there have been eight instances of a team scoring at least two goals in the final and still falling short.
There have been three 1-0 wins: West Germany over Argentina in 1990, Spain over Netherlands in 2010 and Germany over Argentina in 2014. And there was one 0-0 tie in a final; Brazil wound up winning over Italy in a penalty shootout the 1994 final, which had been the only one played on U.S. soil before now.
Back-to-back championships as coach
If Argentina wins on Sunday (July 19, 2026), Lionel Scaloni — who led the team’s push to the 2022 title — would be just the second person to coach two World Cup championship clubs.
Italy went back-to-back as World Cup champions in 1934 and 1938, with both those clubs coached by Vittorio Pozzo.
Nobody else has two titles as a coach. There are three men — Brazil’s Mário Zagallo, West Germany’s Franz Beckenbauer and France’s Didier Deschamps — to have won World Cups as both a player and a coach.
Milestones for Messi
When he takes the field Sunday (July 19, 2026), Argentina star Lionel Messi will be the oldest field player to appear in a World Cup final.
Messi is 39. Sweden’s Gunnar Gren was 37 when he played against Brazil in the 1958 final.
The only player older than Messi to play in a final was Italy goalkeeper Dino Zoff — who was 40 when his club beat West Germany for the 1982 crown. But among field players, Messi will stand alone as the oldest after Sunday.
Messi is also in position to join Brazil’s Cafu as the only person to play in three World Cup men’s finals. And since Cafu was a reserve in one of his appearances, Messi would be the first to start the title game on three occasions.
Records set to tumble with a Spain victory
Youth may be served by Spain in this World Cup final. And if La Roja prevails, experience would also have paid off.
It’s an interesting mix.
Spain is slated to have two teenagers — Lamine Yamal and Pau Cubarsí, a pair of 19-year-olds — play Sunday (July 19) against Argentina in the World Cup final, and no team has ever had more than one teen in the lineup and won the title.
So, that's the power of youth.
Here’s the flip side of that: If Spain wins, coach Luis de la Fuente — who is 65 — would become the oldest World Cup-winning sideline boss, passing Vicente del Bosque, who was 59 when Spain won its first title in 2010.
For the record, the two teenagers enter Sunday (July 19) unbeaten in World Cup finals, according to FIFA, which says only three have ever played in soccer’s biggest game. Pelé was 17 when he helped Brazil win the final over Sweden in 1958, Giuseppe Bergomi was 18 when Italy beat West Germany in 1982, and Kylian Mbappé was 19 when France beat Croatia in 2018.
Argentina and Spain’s winning record
Spain is 28-0-9 in its last 37 matches, which has tied Italy for the longest unbeaten run by a European men’s national team.
Italy also went 28-0-9 from October 2018 through September 2021 — before losing to Spain 2-1 on Oct. 6, 2021 in Milan.
Argentina has a streak worth noting as well. It has won seven consecutive World Cup matches, tied with Italy for the second-longest such run in men's tournament history. Only Brazil, which won 11 straight spanning 2002 and 2006, has a longer World Cup winning streak.
The game is just the second head-to-head meeting in a World Cup between Argentina and Spain. Argentina won 2-1 in the 1966 group stage.
Across all competitions and friendlies, the sides have played 14 times. Each has won six times and they tied twice.

title: Biggest World Cup produces most number of goals
content:
The World Cup has not only grown larger but has also been more prolific.
Ahead of the final, the tournament has produced 307 goals in 103 matches, an average of 2.98 per game, comfortably higher than Qatar 2022, where 172 goals were scored across 64 matches at an average of 2.69.
Sixteen per cent of the goals have come from outside the penalty area, while the Qatar edition had recorded eight percent goals from outside the box.
Former World Cup winner Jurgen Klinsmann, a member of the Technical Study Group, attributed the increase to teams defending deeper and crowding the penalty area, leaving opponents with little choice but to shoot from range.
“There’s no space. Eight, nine players are in the box or outside the box, so it is definitely a recipe for shots from the outside,” the former Germany and United States coach said. “There has been a little bit of a defensive attitude from quite a lot of coaches with a lower block to defend.”
The congestion, Klinsmann added, also makes it harder for goalkeepers to see the ball as it travels through a forest of bodies. “More goals happening from shots from distance, 20-22 yards outside the box through a lot of people, which makes it very difficult for goalkeepers,” he said. “It’s something fun for us coaches to watch. We saw some beautiful goals that way.”
No team has embraced the trend more effectively than Argentina. Lionel Scaloni’s side has scored five goals from outside the penalty area through Lionel Messi (2), Giovani Lo Celso, Julian Alvarez and Enzo Fernandez, equalling the highest tally by any team at a single World Cup since such records have been kept (1966).
In a tournament of nearly three goals per match, some of its most important ones have arrived from the least inviting distances.

title: 20 shots to none: How Spain utterly owned Argentina in World Cup final and rendered Messi irrelevant
content:
For years, Spanish football has been synonymous with control - of the ball, of space, of tempo. On Sunday night at New York New Jersey Stadium, Spain produced perhaps the ultimate expression of that philosophy, reducing defending champions Argentina and 
Lionel Messi to mere passengers in the FIFA World Cup 2026 final.
Ferran Torres' 106th-minute winner secured a 1-0 victory after extra time and delivered Spain's second 
FIFA World Cup title. But the scoreline barely hinted at the gulf between the two sides. This was not simply a victory. It was a footballing masterclass that left Argentina chasing shadows for almost two hours.
The numbers told the story as emphatically as the result. Spain unleashed 20 shots, 12 of them on target, completed 738 of their 817 passes and forced Emiliano Martínez into one save after another. Argentina, by contrast, failed to muster a single shot in regular time.
That extraordinary statistic places the 2026 final in unique company. According to Opta, among nearly 900 World Cup matches dating back to 1966, only three teams have failed to attempt a shot during normal time: Costa Rica against Brazil in 1990, Costa Rica against Spain in 2022, and now Argentina against Spain in the 2026 final. The first two were underdogs expected to survive. 
The third was the reigning world champion led by Messi.
Spain ensured there was never a platform for Argentina to settle. Fabian Ruiz, later Pedri, dictated the rhythm from midfield, Alex Beana and Rodri constantly found pockets between the lines, while Dani Elmo and, later Nico Williams Jr, and Lamine Yamal stretched the pitch with relentless width. Argentina spent most of the night retreating, reorganising and defending.
Messi, who had carried Argentina through another deep World Cup run at the age of 39, found himself isolated. Every touch was immediately met by red shirts, every passing lane closed, every attempt to slow the game and impose his rhythm suffocated almost before it began. It was one of the quietest nights of his World Cup career - not because he lacked ambition, but because Spain never allowed the contest to be played on his terms.
If anything illustrated Spain's control, it was Argentina's inability to escape their own half. The South Americans finished the 90 minutes with just 34% possession - their lowest share of the ball in any World Cup match since Opta's records began in 1966.
Even more revealing, goalkeeper Emiliano Martínez finished as player with most touches for Argentina, underlining how relentlessly Spain pinned the holders back. Spain did not merely outplay Argentina; they monopolised the ball, dictated the territory and deprived the champions of the rhythm that had defined their title defence.
Only Martínez prevented the contest from ending much earlier. The Aston Villa goalkeeper produced a string of outstanding saves to deny Williams, Pedri, and Torres, repeatedly frustrating Spain as wave after wave of attacks crashed into Argentina's penalty area.
Eventually, however, even Martínez could not withstand the pressure.
Sixteen minutes into extra time, Williams managed to cushion a header into the path of Torres, who fired his finish beyond the goalkeeper. It was a goal Spain had threatened all evening and one that finally rewarded their dominance.
Argentina's hopes had taken a major dent when Enzo Fernández was sent off in stoppage time, reducing Lionel Scaloni's side to 10 men and extinguishing any realistic prospect of an Argentine surge. Even before the red card, though, the balance of the contest had long been decided.
Spain's triumph was built not on moments of brilliance alone but on collective superiority. Luis de la Fuente's side controlled possession, dictated territory and suffocated every avenue Argentina attempted to explore.
Their pressing denied transitions, their midfield recycled possession with remarkable composure and their defensive line gave Messi and Julián Álvarez little room to breathe.
For Argentina, the defeat marked the end of a reign that began in Qatar four years ago. Whether this ultimately proves to be Messi's final World Cup appearance remains to be seen, but if it is, it closed not with one last moment of genius but with Spain rendering football's greatest player almost anonymous.
World Cup finals are often remembered for drama, controversy or moments of individual inspiration. This one will be remembered for something far rarer - complete domination.
Spain didn't just beat the defending champions. They made them disappear.

title: Lionel Messi exits FIFA World Cup in tears and as football's greatest
content:
Tears rolled down 
Lionel Messi's face as he walked away with a silver medal around his neck. Spain had beaten Argentina 1-0 after extra time in the 
FIFA World Cup final, ending the defending champions' title defence and what could well be the last World Cup appearance of the greatest footballer the game has seen.
Twenty years after making his World Cup debut, the 39-year-old could not produce one more miracle. Argentina, chasing back-to-back World Cup titles, were outplayed by Spain for long periods at MetLife Stadium, where 80,663 spectators had largely turned up hoping to watch their Messi-ah lift the trophy once again.
Instead, it was Spain celebrating their second World Cup title.
Ferran Torres scored just 37 seconds into the second half of extra time to settle the final after Spain had dominated the contest from the opening whistle. As Spanish players celebrated, Messi stood with his teammates, struggling to hold back tears while Argentina's supporters applauded them from the stands.
Messi, head bowed, hands on his hips, watched 19-year-old Lamine Yamal embrace Torres after the winning goal. Moments later, with tears running down his cheeks, he collected his runners-up medal while Spain celebrated amid confetti and fireworks.
"I hope that everyone feels proud of him, of what he’s achieved because he’s the best football player ever stepping foot on a pitch," Argentina coach Lionel Scaloni said after the match.
The statistics reflected Spain's dominance in the final. Luis de la Fuente's side outshot Argentina 20-2 and completed 845 passes to Argentina's 433. According to Opta, the 18-shot difference was the second biggest in a World Cup final since records began in 1966, behind only West Germany's 23-1 shot advantage over Argentina in the 1990 final.
Spain also succeeded where few teams have ever managed to -- they kept Messi quiet.
"The first idea was to keep Messi at bay," Spain coach Luis de la Fuente said.
Messi managed only 15 touches in the first half and finished with 54 touches in the match. He never found the space to create the moments that had carried Argentina through the tournament. His passes into dangerous areas failed to connect and he rarely received the ball in positions from where he could threaten Spain's defence.
 That had not been the story of Argentina's campaign until the final.
After leaving European football for Major League Soccer three years ago, some questioned whether Messi's best days were behind him. Instead, he led Argentina back to another World Cup final with eight goals and four assists.
His eight goals helped Argentina through the group stage and knockout rounds. Against England in the semifinal, he set up both goals in a stoppage-time victory. Against Egypt, with Argentina trailing 2-0 and facing elimination, he provided the cross for the first goal before scoring the equaliser himself.
Throughout the tournament, Messi once again showed his ability to influence matches despite covering less ground than his teammates. He walked for nearly half the time he spent on the pitch, averaging around five kilometres per game, conserving energy before producing bursts of quality whenever Argentina needed him.
Against Spain, though, that moment never arrived.
If this was his last, then Messi ends his World Cup career with 21 goals, a record that stood until France's Kylian Mbappe scored twice in the third-place playoff to move to 22. He also became the first player to start three World Cup finals and has 125 international goals, second only to Cristiano Ronaldo's 146.
His achievements extend far beyond statistics.
World Cup winner in 2022. Consecutive Copa América titles. Four UEFA Champions League trophies. Twelve league titles across spells with Barcelona and Paris Saint-Germain, only player in football history to win 8 Ballon d'Or awards. A career that placed him alongside 
Diego Maradona and Pelé in football's greatest-ever debates.
As the final whistle blew, Spanish players queued up to console him. Several hugged him before Argentina collected their silver medals. When Spain formed a guard of honour for the runners-up, Messi tried to smile but could not. His expression barely changed as opponents patted him on the back while he walked to the podium.
If this was indeed his final appearance on football's biggest stage, it ended without the fairytale finish many had hoped for.
It was not because Messi failed to try. Spain simply gave him no room to operate. Their Rodri-led midfield controlled possession, limited Argentina's attacks and prevented Messi from dictating the game the way he had done throughout the tournament.
The final may not have delivered the ending he wanted, but it does little to change how his career will be remembered.
It may not have been the ending Messi imagined. It may not even be the end. Whether this proves to be Messi's final World Cup appearance or not will become clear only when he decides. But if this was the last image of him on football's biggest stage, it was one of tears, not triumph.
However, the defeat in New Jersey cannot undo a career that has already secured its place among the greatest the sport has known. If this is to be his tearful sign off, fans will still look back at having been blessed by football's greatest of all time.

title: Revealed: The eye-watering amount Spain players will earn for winning the 2026 FIFA World Cup
content:
Spain's triumph at the 2026 
FIFA World Cup did not only bring the nation's second world title. It also secured one of the biggest financial rewards ever distributed to a World Cup-winning squad after FIFA increased the tournament's prize money to record levels.
Luis de la Fuente's side defeated Argentina 1-0 after extra time in the final at the New York New Jersey Stadium, with Ferran Torres scoring the decisive goal in the 106th minute. The victory earned the Royal Spanish Football Federation (RFEF) the tournament's top prize of $51 million from FIFA.
According to reports, Spain's players will each receive a substantial share of that prize under a pre-agreed bonus arrangement negotiated between the squad captains and the RFEF.
How much each Spain player will receive
FIFA awards federations a single payment once their World Cup campaign concludes, with the amount determined by their final finishing position rather than after each knockout round.
By merely reaching the World Cup final, Spain had already guaranteed the RFEF $34 million.
Under the agreement reported by AS, 40 per cent of that guaranteed prize money would have been allocated to the players. That would have created a dressing-room bonus pool worth approximately $13.6 million, equating to just over $523,000 for each member of Spain's 26-man squad before taxes.
Winning the World Cup, however, significantly increased those figures.
After lifting the trophy, Spain's players became entitled to 45 per cent of FIFA's winners' prize.
That means approximately $22.95 million will be shared between the 26 players, with each squad member expected to receive around $882,700 before taxes, 
according to AS.The remainder of FIFA's prize money will stay with the RFEF, which will use part of the windfall to cover the costs of Spain's World Cup campaign while also benefiting financially from one of the most lucrative tournaments in its history.
Argentina receive runners-up prize
Although Argentina fell short of retaining the World Cup after Ferran Torres' extra-time winner, Lionel Scaloni's side will still receive a substantial runners-up payment.
FIFA will award the Argentine Football Association $34 million for finishing second.
Unlike Spain, however, no official details have yet emerged regarding how that prize money will be distributed among Argentina's 26-player squad.
 Argentina walks on the pitch after their loss in the World Cup final soccer match between Spain and Argentina in East Rutherford, N.J., near New York, Sunday, July 19, 2026. (AP Photo/Julio Cortez)
Record prize money across the tournament
The 2026 World Cup featured the largest prize fund in the competition's history, with FIFA increasing overall financial rewards by around 50 per cent compared with previous editions.
Prize money based on final finishing positions is as follows:
- Champions - Spain: $51 million
- Runners-up - Argentina: $34 million
- Third place - England: $30 million
- Fourth place - France: $28 million
- Quarter-finalists (5th-8th): Switzerland, Norway, Belgium, Morocco: $20 million each
- Round of 16 (9th-16th): Colombia, Egypt, United States, Portugal, Mexico, Brazil, Paraguay and Canada: $16 million each
- Last 32 (17th-32nd): Ghana, Algeria, Australia, Cape Verde, Austria, Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Senegal, DR Congo, Ecuador, Sweden, Ivory Coast, Netherlands, Germany, Japan and South Africa: $12 million each
- Group stage (33rd-48th): Uruguay, Scotland, South Korea, Iran, Uzbekistan, Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Curaçao, Czech Republic, Qatar, Panama, Jordan, Tunisia, New Zealand, Haiti and Turkey: $10 million each
Those performance-based payments account for $703 million of FIFA's total $871 million tournament prize fund.
The remaining $168 million is distributed among participating national associations regardless of where they finish, ensuring every nation receives additional financial support for taking part in football's biggest tournament.
For Spain, however, victory delivered far more than just the World Cup trophy. Alongside a place in football history, every member of the squad is set to receive one of the largest bonuses ever awarded to a Spanish national team, reflecting both FIFA's record prize fund and the agreement reached between the players and the RFEF.

title: Spain dethrone Argentina to win World Cup! Torres delivers extra-time heartbreak to Messi's side
content:
Spain have won their second 
FIFA World Cup title after Ferran Torres struck a late extra-time winner to break Argentina’s resistance in a dramatic final. 
Lionel Messi’s 10-man side fell short of becoming the first team since Brazil in 1962 to successfully defend the World Cup crown.
Spain finally found the breakthrough after a tense and dramatic contest as Torres scored in the 106th minute to hand La Roja a 1-0 victory over defending champions Argentina and deny Messi another World Cup fairytale.
After 105 minutes of relentless pressure, missed chances and heroic Argentine resistance, Torres delivered the decisive blow just 37 seconds after the restart for the second period of extra time.
The goal came from a brilliant team move. Pedro Porro sent a deep cross into the box, Nico Williams headed the ball back into the danger area, and Torres arrived late to smash the finish into the roof of the net.
Spain’s celebrations erupted as Argentina’s hopes of becoming the first team since Brazil in 1962 to retain the World Cup title were finally ended.
The goal was a reward for Spain's dominance throughout the contest. La Roja controlled possession, created the majority of chances and repeatedly tested Argentina goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez, who had kept his side alive with a record-breaking performance in the final.
Argentina, who were reduced to 10 men after Enzo Fernandez's late red card in normal time, were left chasing the game with a numerical disadvantage and little attacking threat.
 Messi's final World Cup ends in heartbreak as Argentina fight with 10 men
The final appeared destined for penalties after both teams failed to break the deadlock in 90 minutes and the first half of extra time.
Spain had even seen a goal ruled out earlier in extra time when Nico Williams found the net, but the referee ruled that Mikel Merino had fouled Nicolas Otamendi before the finish.
However, Argentina's resistance suffered a major blow before extra time when Fernandez was sent off in stoppage time after receiving a second yellow card for a late challenge on Pau Cubarsi.
The midfielder, who scored Argentina's equaliser in their semifinal win over England, protested the decision but referee Slavko Vincic showed no hesitation.
With 10 players on the pitch, Argentina were forced into a defensive approach. Coach Lionel Scaloni even replaced Julian Alvarez with defender Marcos Senesi as Spain continued to push forward.
The defeat ended Argentina's dream of a fourth World Cup title and denied Messi the chance to lift the trophy for a second consecutive tournament.
Messi, playing in what was widely expected to be his final World Cup appearance, struggled to influence the match as Spain's midfield and defence successfully contained the Argentine superstar.
The defeat also ended Argentina's remarkable run as reigning champions after their dramatic penalty shootout victory over France in the 2022 final in Qatar.
Spain, meanwhile, completed a golden era revival by securing their second World Cup title after their famous 2010 triumph in South Africa.
The victory also capped an extraordinary tournament for teenage sensation Lamine Yamal, who became the first player to feature in both a UEFA European Championship final and a FIFA World Cup final as a teenager.

title: Lionel Messi, Lamine Yamal share emotional hug after Spain's World Cup triumph
content:
NEW DELHI: 
Lionel Messi and Spain youngster Lamine Yamal shared a heartfelt hug after the final whistle of the FIFA World Cup 2026 final on Sunday, giving fans one of the most memorable moments of the tournament.
After Spain beat defending champions Argentina 1-0 in extra time to win the title, Messi and Yamal met on the pitch, embraced each other and had a brief chat. The touching moment quickly went viral on social media.
Their hug carried extra meaning because of their famous connection from the 2007 UNICEF photoshoot, when a young Messi was photographed with baby Yamal.
At 39, Messi became only the second footballer to play in three 
FIFA World Cup finals, while 19-year-old Yamal celebrated winning the World Cup in his first final appearance.
Torres scores title-winning goal
Spain secured their second FIFA World Cup title thanks to substitute Ferran Torres, who scored the only goal of the match in the 106th minute of extra time.
The move began just after the restart of the second period of extra time. Argentina were already down to 10 men after Enzo Fernandez was sent off in the 93rd minute.
Lamine Yamal collected the ball on the right wing and passed it to Pedro Porro. Porro then delivered a cross into the penalty area that was almost going out of play. 
Nico Williams, who had come on in the 75th minute, headed the ball back into the middle, where Torres reacted quickly and fired a left-footed shot past goalkeeper Emiliano Martínez.
It was Spain's 20th shot of the match, while Argentina had still not managed a single attempt at goal.
'It was a goal scored by 47 million people'
Torres said the winning goal belonged to the entire country and reflected on the criticism he had faced during the tournament.
“I think at the end of the day, it was a goal scored by 47 million people,” Torres said. “I feel very happy. I was heavily criticized during the World Cup, but I think it was written in destiny and things. But I always have that chance, that option to continue, and God gives it to those who believe it, who deserve it most.”
After scoring, the Barcelona forward celebrated by kicking down the corner flag before being surrounded by his teammates as Spain's fans erupted in celebration.
Spain complete dominant performance
Spain controlled the match for long periods and created chance after chance before finally breaking Argentina's resistance in extra time.
Marc Cucurella started the move from the back by passing to Eric García, who found Martín Zubimendi. The midfielder then released Yamal on the right wing, leading to the move that eventually produced Torres' winner.
Torres almost added a second goal later in the match but was ruled offside. Spain then defended Argentina's late corner kicks to seal a famous victory and lift the World Cup trophy for the second time.

title: Messi branded a ‘baby’ after desperate failed attempt to get Cucurella sent off in World Cup final defeat
content:
Lionel Messi found himself at the centre of an unusual flashpoint during Argentina's 1-0 extra-time defeat to Spain in the FIFA World Cup 2026 final after appearing to urge referee Slavko Vinčić to punish Marc Cucurella for covering his mouth while speaking.
The incident came moments after Argentina had been reduced to 10 men. Midfielder Enzo Fernández, who had already been booked for dissent earlier in the match, received a second yellow card in stoppage time of normal time following a late challenge on Spain defender Pau Cubarsí, leaving Lionel Scaloni's side facing the closing stages with a numerical disadvantage.
Messi appeals over Cucurella exchange
As Argentina players surrounded Slovenian referee Vinčić to protest the dismissal, Spain defender Cucurella briefly approached Messi during the stoppage. Television cameras showed the left-back speaking while partially covering his mouth with his hand before lowering it almost immediately.
Messi was then seen animately gesturing towards the referee, appearing to indicate that Cucurella should also be punished under FIFA's recently introduced regulation concerning players covering their mouths during confrontations.
FIFA rule explained as no action taken
The law has become one of the most closely scrutinised changes at this World Cup. 
Earlier in the tournament, Paraguay's Miguel Almirón became the first player dismissed under the regulation, while Ecuador defender Piero Hincapié was also shown a red card after covering his mouth during an exchange with an opponent.
However, the regulation does not make every instance of covering the mouth an automatic sending-off. FIFA's guidance allows referees to consider the nature of the interaction, with sanctions applying when the action forms part of an inappropriate or confrontational exchange. In Cucurella's case, Vinčić took no action after determining the threshold for a dismissal had not been met.
Pundits criticise Messi's reaction
The moment nevertheless drew immediate criticism from several television pundits, who viewed Messi's appeal as an attempt to level the numbers after Argentina's dismissal.
Speaking on ITV, former England defender Lee Dixon was particularly critical.
"The game has gone if you're getting bookings and red cards for that, to get somebody sent off," Dixon said during the live broadcast.
"You shouldn't be such a baby to try and get someone sent off for that; it's ridiculous."
BBC Sport presenter Gabby Logan also described the incident as "desperation from Messi", while former England captain Wayne Rooney echoed that assessment.
"It is," Rooney said. "Argentina play like that, we know that's what they do. The one thing you want is good sportsmanship. That was sad to see, Lionel Messi doing that."
Former England goalkeeper Joe Hart shared a similar view, adding: "Yeah, I didn't enjoy that at all from Messi. He realises how on top Spain are in this final, Gabby, if even Lionel Messi's reverting to that."
Spain secure victory as Messi bows out
Ultimately, Messi's protests had no impact on the outcome. Spain continued to control proceedings before substitute Ferran Torres scored the only goal of the match in extra time, securing a 1-0 victory and Spain's second FIFA World Cup title.
 Spain's Marc Cucurella (24) celebrates in front of Argentina's Lionel Messi (10) after the World Cup final soccer match between Spain and Argentina in East Rutherford, N.J., near New York, Sunday, July 19, 2026. (AP Photo/Matt Rourke)
For Messi, who was widely expected to be making his final World Cup appearance at the age of 39, the evening ended in disappointment as Argentina surrendered their crown, while the debate surrounding the brief exchange with Cucurella became one of the final talking points of the tournament.

title: Awkward! Trump remains on podium during trophy celebrations; Infantino steps in
content:
NEW DELHI: US President 
Donald Trump became one of the biggest talking points after Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 to win the FIFA World Cup 2026 final at New York New Jersey Stadium on Sunday.
Trump stood alongside the Spain players as they received the World Cup trophy and remained on the podium during the celebrations. 
 Trump only stepped aside after FIFA president Gianni Infantino pulled him away, allowing captain Rodri to lift the trophy with his teammates. The moment quickly went viral on social media.
The US President also handed the FIFA World Cup trophy to the Spanish players after the final at New York New Jersey Stadium.
Boos greet Trump during trophy ceremony
Boos echoed around the stadium when Trump and Infantino walked onto the field for the trophy presentation after the match. 
Trump, who had arrived at the stadium by helicopter, congratulated players with handshakes as they walked across the stage before standing with Spain's squad during the trophy presentation.
Torres fires Spain to second World Cup title
Spain beat defending champions Argentina 1-0 after extra time thanks to substitute Ferran Torres' goal in the 106th minute.
Spain dominated possession and created far more chances throughout the match, while 
Lionel Messi's Argentina managed to force extra time before finally conceding.
Torres reacted quickest to a loose ball inside the box and fired a left-footed shot under the crossbar to seal Spain's second FIFA World Cup title, their first since 2010.
Trump's earlier World Cup controversy
Trump had earlier found himself at the centre of controversy during the tournament after calling FIFA president Gianni Infantino to review the red card shown to US striker Folarin Balogun in the group-stage match against Bosnia and Herzegovina.
Balogun's one-match suspension was later lifted, allowing him to play in the Round of 16 against Belgium, where the United States lost 4-1.

title: 'I can't believe it': Ferran Torres reacts after firing Spain to World Cup glory
content:
Spain's World Cup final hero Ferran Torres admitted he was still struggling to believe what he had achieved after scoring the extra-time winner that sealed a historic 1-0 victory over Lionel Messi's Argentina in the FIFA World Cup 2026 final.
The Barcelona forward, introduced from the bench by coach Luis de la Fuente, etched his name into Spanish football folklore with the decisive strike in the 106th minute at MetLife Stadium. Moments after the final whistle, an emotional Torres confessed that the magnitude of the occasion had not yet sunk in.
 "Honestly, I can't believe it. I'm just trying to enjoy it, but I don't know what's happening," Torres said in an interview with the host broadcaster, in a video shared by DAZN Football on X. "I didn't think too much. I just saw the ball coming to me and I just shot it with the power of all the Spanish people."
The goal crowned Spain world champions for only the second time after their maiden triumph in 2010 and ended Argentina's hopes of becoming the first nation since Brazil in 1962 to successfully defend the FIFA World Cup.
'The goal was for 47 million people'
Torres later dedicated his match-winning strike to the people of Spain, insisting the triumph belonged to the entire country rather than just the squad.
"The goal was for 47 million people; it wasn't just mine, or of the 26 players here," Torres said, as quoted by FIFA. 
"I believe today's outcome was written in the stars. The game was there for us to win. We were far from our people, but we did our best to make them feel as close as possible."
Spain had dominated the contest from the opening whistle, controlling possession, creating the overwhelming majority of chances and restricting Argentina to almost nothing in attack. Yet it took until extra time for La Roja to finally break through.
Just 37 seconds into the second period of extra time, Pedro Porro floated in a deep cross that Nico Williams brilliantly headed back across goal. Torres timed his run perfectly before smashing a left-footed finish into the roof of the net beyond Emiliano Martinez, whose 12 saves had kept Argentina alive for more than 100 minutes.
Facing Messi on the biggest stage
Torres also acknowledged the unique pressure of facing Lionel Messi in what is expected to be the Argentina captain's final World Cup appearance.
"All finals are difficult. When Messi is on the opposition team that nervousness always creeps in," Torres admitted. "But ultimately we've always stuck to our guns, to playing our own brand of football, and I think today we've shown that once again."
Spain's disciplined display completed one of the most dominant World Cup campaigns in recent memory. Luis de la Fuente's side finished the tournament unbeaten, conceding just one goal in eight matches while adding the FIFA World Cup title to the UEFA Euro 2024 crown.

title: FIFA World Cup 2026 awards: Full winners list as Rodri, Mbappe claim top honours
content:
Spain's historic FIFA World Cup 2026 triumph was reflected in the tournament's individual honours as Rodri, Unai Simon and Pau Cubarsi swept three of the biggest awards, while France captain 
Kylian Mbappe claimed the Golden Boot after another sensational goalscoring campaign.
Following Spain's 1-0 extra-time victory over Argentina in the final at MetLife Stadium, FIFA announced the winners of its prestigious post-tournament awards, recognising the standout performers of the biggest World Cup in history.
 Rodri was named the recipient of the Golden Ball after producing a series of commanding displays in midfield throughout Spain's unbeaten title-winning run. The Manchester City midfielder dictated the tempo in almost every game and capped off a remarkable tournament by helping La Roja secure their second 
FIFA World Cup crown.
Goalkeeper Unai Simon added another trophy to Spain's celebrations after winning the Golden Glove Award. Simon was the backbone of Spain's formidable defence, keeping seven clean sheets in eight matches as the champions conceded just one goal throughout the tournament. He also set a World Cup record by going 650 consecutive minutes without conceding.
Teenage sensation Pau Cubarsi completed Spain's awards sweep by winning the FIFA Best Young Player Award. 
The 19-year-old defender emerged as one of the tournament's biggest stars with a string of composed performances at the heart of Spain's defence, playing a crucial role in the team's record-setting defensive campaign.
Mbappe wins Golden Boot despite France's semifinal exit
France captain Kylian Mbappe once again finished as the tournament's leading scorer, collecting the Golden Boot after scoring 10 goals in eight matches.
Although France fell short of reaching a third consecutive World Cup final, Mbappe's remarkable campaign saw him become the first player to win the World Cup Golden Boot in back-to-back editions. He also extended his record as the highest goalscorer in men's FIFA World Cup history with 22 career goals.
Meanwhile, the Netherlands were presented with the FIFA Fair Play Award in recognition of their exemplary discipline and sportsmanship throughout the competition.
Full list of FIFA World Cup 2026 award winners
- Golden Ball: Rodri (Spain)
- Silver Ball: Lionel Messi (Argentina)
- Bronze Ball: Kylian Mbappe (France)
- Golden Boot: Kylian Mbappe (France)
- Silver Boot: Lionel Messi (Argentina)
- Bronze Boot: Jude Bellingham (England)
- Golden Glove: Unai Simon (Spain)
- FIFA Young Player Award: Pau Cubarsi (Spain)
- FIFA Fair Play Trophy: The Netherlands
Spain capped a memorable tournament by defeating defending champions Argentina 1-0 after extra time in the final, with substitute Ferran Torres scoring the decisive goal in the 106th minute. The victory secured Spain's second FIFA World Cup title and completed a near-perfect campaign, with La Roja conceding just one goal across eight matches while dominating both the team and individual honours.

title: Mbappé scripts World Cup history as first player to win back-to-back Golden Boots
content:
Kylian Mbappé made 
FIFA World Cup history by becoming the first player ever to win the Golden Boot at two consecutive tournaments after finishing as the leading scorer at the 2026 edition, edging 
Lionel Messi in one of the closest races the competition has seen.
The France captain finished with 10 goals in eight matches, while also providing four assists, to claim the tournament's top scorer award. His closest challenger, Argentina captain Lionel Messi, ended the World Cup with eight goals and four assists after failing to find the net in Sunday's 1-0 extra-time defeat to Spain in the final.
Messi entered the showpiece knowing he needed a hat-trick to overtake Mbappé after the Frenchman struck twice in France's dramatic 6-4 defeat to England in Saturday's third-place playoff. Instead, Spain's disciplined midfield, led by Golden Ball winner Rodri, kept Argentina's captain quiet as La Roja lifted their second World Cup title.
Mbappé's bronze match changed the race
The Golden Boot race appeared destined to go down to the final after Messi had briefly moved ahead following Argentina's semifinal victory over England.
Although both stars were level on eight goals at that stage, Messi's four assists placed him ahead of Mbappé, who had three, under FIFA's tiebreak rules.
Everything changed in the third-place playoff.
France found themselves trailing England 4-0 at halftime before Mbappé sparked an unlikely comeback, scoring in the 48th and 66th minutes to take his tournament tally to 10 goals. Those two strikes not only moved him clear of Messi in the scoring charts but also saw him surpass the Argentine as the all-time leading men's World Cup goalscorer with 22 career goals.
England eventually won the extraordinary contest 6-4, but Mbappé's individual achievements ensured he headed into the final day of the tournament in pole position for another Golden Boot.
Messi unable to mount one final charge
All eyes then turned to the World Cup final at the New York New Jersey Stadium.
Argentina needed inspiration from Messi against a Spain side that had conceded just once throughout the tournament, but Luis de la Fuente's men produced one of the competition's most complete defensive performances.
 Argentina's Lionel Messi (10) cries at the end of the World Cup final soccer match between Spain and Argentina in East Rutherford, N.J., near New York, Sunday, July 19, 2026. (AP Photo/Stephanie Scarbrough)
Rodri dominated midfield, while Spain restricted Argentina to no shots on target during regular time. Messi struggled to influence proceedings and finished the match without adding to either his goal or assist tally.
Spain eventually secured a 1-0 victory after extra time to end Argentina's reign as world champions.
The result also confirmed Mbappé as the Golden Boot winner, with Messi finishing second after another outstanding World Cup campaign that yielded eight goals and four assists.
Another chapter in Mbappé's remarkable World Cup career
The award further strengthened Mbappé's extraordinary record on football's biggest stage.
After winning the Golden Boot at Qatar 2022 with eight goals, the Real Madrid forward returned four years later to score 10 more, becoming the first player to claim the honour in consecutive World Cups.
His tally also made him the first player to reach double figures in a single World Cup since Germany legend Gerd Müller scored 10 goals in 1970.
Only France's Just Fontaine, who scored 13 goals in 1958, and Hungary's Sándor Kocsis, who netted 11 in 1954, have managed more goals in a single World Cup tournament.
Across the 2018, 2022 and 2026 editions, Mbappé has now scored 22 World Cup goals in just 22 appearances, overtaking Messi to become the competition's all-time leading men's goalscorer.
Other players also featured prominently in the Golden Boot race, including England's Harry Kane, who scored six goals, as well as Jude Bellingham and Norway's Erling Haaland, who both finished with seven goals, all of whom were among the leading scorers during the tournament.
Final individual honours
While Mbappé collected the Golden Boot, Spain dominated the remaining individual awards after lifting the trophy.
Rodri was named the adidas Golden Ball winner after controlling Spain's midfield throughout the tournament, culminating in a masterful display in the final. The Manchester City midfielder finished the competition with a World Cup record 694 completed passes and was recognised as the tournament's best player ahead of Messi, Mbappé and England's Jude Bellingham.
Spain goalkeeper Unai Simón claimed the Golden Glove after conceding just one goal across eight matches and keeping seven clean sheets during La Roja's title-winning campaign.
Although Messi fell short of both the World Cup trophy and the Golden Boot in what could prove to be his final appearance at football's biggest tournament, his campaign remained one of the finest of the competition. The Argentina captain contributed directly to 12 goals through eight strikes and four assists while leading the defending champions to a second consecutive World Cup final.
This time, however, the tournament belonged to Mbappé.
With a record-breaking 10-goal campaign, another Golden Boot and a place alone at the top of the World Cup's all-time scoring charts, the France captain once again underlined why he remains one of the defining players of his generation.

title: Who is Ferran Torres? Spain's World Cup final hero who broke Argentina's heart
content:
Spain's long wait for a breakthrough in the 
FIFA World Cup 2026 final finally ended through an unlikely hero. Substitute Ferran Torres scored the decisive goal in extra time to hand Spain a dramatic 1-0 victory over defending champions Argentina, sealing the nation's second World Cup title and ending 
Lionel Messi's dream of retaining the crown.
Torres struck in the 106th minute, just 37 seconds into the second period of extra time, capping off a dominant Spanish display that had somehow remained goalless through 90 minutes despite relentless pressure.
Torres delivers when Spain needed him most
The winning goal came from a flowing Spanish move. Pedro Porro floated a deep cross into the penalty area, Nico Williams intelligently headed the ball back across goal, and Torres timed his run perfectly before smashing a left-footed finish into the roof of the net.
The strike finally broke Argentina's stubborn resistance after goalkeeper Emiliano Martinez had produced a series of outstanding saves to keep his side in the contest.
Spain completely controlled proceedings, finishing with 15 shots in regulation compared to Argentina's none, while also earning nine corners to Argentina's one. Lionel Scaloni's side had managed to survive until extra time despite spending long spells without the ball.
Argentina's task became even harder after Enzo Fernandez was sent off in second-half stoppage time for a second yellow card following a reckless challenge on Pau Cubarsi. Reduced to 10 men, the defending champions were unable to recover once Torres found the breakthrough.
The victory also saw Spain create history by becoming the first nation to simultaneously hold both the men's and women's FIFA World Cup titles.
Who is Ferran Torres?
Ferran Torres García is a Spanish international forward who plays for Barcelona and the Spain national team. Comfortable as both a winger and a central striker, Torres has built a reputation for intelligent movement, clinical finishing and delivering in crucial moments.
The 26-year-old began his professional career with Valencia, making his senior debut in 2017 before earning a move to Manchester City in 2020. During his spell in England, he won the Premier League and the EFL Cup under Pep Guardiola before joining Barcelona in January 2022.
Although Torres initially faced criticism and inconsistency after arriving at Camp Nou, he transformed his fortunes over the following seasons. He played a vital role in Barcelona's domestic treble-winning campaign in 2024-25 and followed it up with his most prolific scoring season, netting 21 goals to help the Catalan giants reclaim the La Liga title.
On the international stage, Torres progressed through Spain's youth ranks before making his senior debut in 2020. He represented La Roja at UEFA Euro 2020, the 2022 FIFA World Cup and UEFA Euro 2024, where Spain lifted the European Championship.
Now, Torres has etched his name into Spanish football folklore. His extra-time winner against Argentina not only secured Spain's second FIFA World Cup title but also ensured he will forever be remembered as the man who denied Lionel Messi and Argentina a historic back-to-back World Cup triumph.
